metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = floor / ground state / lower bound; CRIMSON = trial state / estimate above ground state
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: no eigenbasis-expansion proof, no helium integral, no Rayleigh-Ritz matrix
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Guess any wave function you like for the helium atom — any shape at all. Compute the energy your guess predicts. It will always land at or above the true ground-state energy. Never below.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This is Liam, in for Bear. Guess any wave function you like for the helium atom — any shape at all. Compute the energy your guess predicts. It will always land

beats.narration_text: In 1929, Hylleraas wrote down a trial wave function for helium with one free parameter. He minimized it to get minus 77.5 electron volts. The exact answer was minus 78.98. He was off by about 2% — and he knew with certainty that he was wrong in the right direction.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: In 1929, Hylleraas wrote down a trial wave function for helium with one free parameter. He minimized it to get minus 77.5 electron volts. The exact answer was m

beats.narration_text: Any trial wave function gives an energy above or equal to the true ground state. Push your estimate down as far as it will go, and you get the best possible ceiling on the answer. Why can't any guessed wave function beat the ground state?

props.headline: Any trial wave function gives an energy above or equal to…

card.headline: Any guess lands above the true ground state. Why?
card.dek: Why can't a trial wave function give an energy below the true ground-state energy?

beats.narration_text: The issue is superposition. Any quantum state can be written as a combination of the true energy eigenstates — the ground state, the first excited state, the second, and so on.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The issue is superposition. Any quantum state can be written as a combination of the true energy eigenstates — the ground state, the first excited state, the se

beats.narration_text: When you compute the energy of your trial state, you're computing a weighted average of those eigenstate energies. The weights are the squared magnitudes of the expansion coefficients. They're all non-negative and they sum to one.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: When you compute the energy of your trial state, you're computing a weighted average of those eigenstate energies. The weights are the squared magnitudes of the

beats.narration_text: That average energy is a sum of the form: weight on the ground state times its energy, plus weight on the first excited state times its higher energy, plus more terms above that.

props.headline: That average energy is a sum of the form: weight on…

build.suggested: suggested prompt: That average energy is a sum of the form: weight on the ground state times its energy, plus weight on the first excited state times its higher energy, plus more

beats.narration_text: Now here is the key step. Every energy in that sum is at least as large as the ground-state energy — by definition, E-zero is the lowest. So every term in the weighted sum is at least as large as the ground-state energy times its weight.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Now here is the key step. Every energy in that sum is at least as large as the ground-state energy — by definition, E-zero is the lowest. So every term in the w

beats.narration_text: The total weighted average is therefore at least E-zero times the sum of all the weights — and since all weights sum to one, the average energy is at least E-zero. The floor is mathematically inescapable.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The total weighted average is therefore at least E-zero times the sum of all the weights — and since all weights sum to one, the average energy is at least E-ze

beats.narration_text: So the variational method is: tune parameters in your trial function to push the average energy down as far as it will go. Whatever minimum you find is a rigorous upper bound on the ground-state energy. The lower you push it, the tighter the bound.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: So the variational method is: tune parameters in your trial function to push the average energy down as far as it will go. Whatever minimum you find is a rigoro

beats.narration_text: The floor is E-zero. You can approach it. You cannot go below.

props.headline: The floor is E-zero. You can approach it. You cannot go…

card.headline: E_trial >= E_0 always. Minimize to get the tightest bound.
card.dek: The weighted average of eigenstate energies cannot fall below the smallest ingredient.

beats.narration_text: This one-sided guarantee is what makes the variational method trustworthy. Other approximations can overshoot or undershoot in unknown directions. Here, you always know which side you're on: above the truth.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: This one-sided guarantee is what makes the variational method trustworthy. Other approximations can overshoot or undershoot in unknown directions. Here, you alw

beats.narration_text: That's also the sanity check: if your variational energy somehow falls below the known exact ground-state energy, you've made an error — a sign mistake, a normalization error. The theorem does not bend. The whole of modern quantum chemistry runs on this one inequality.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: That's also the sanity check: if your variational energy somehow falls below the known exact ground-state energy, you've made an error — a sign mistake, a norma

beats.narration_text: Illustrative: for hydrogen, the exact ground state is minus 0.5 Hartree. With four Gaussian basis functions as trial states, the variational calculation gives minus 0.4993 Hartree — 0.14% above the exact answer. With more basis functions the result converges from above. Every step lands on or above minus 0.5 Hartree. The floor holds.

beats.narration_text: The question: why can't a trial wave function give an energy below the true ground state? Because any state is a superposition of eigenstates, and the average of numbers each at or above E-zero can never fall below E-zero. Quantum mechanics.

card.headline: E_trial >= E_0. The floor holds for every guess.
card.dek: Weighted average of eigenstate energies can't fall below the smallest one.
